item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The discussion was a roller-coaster ride yet actually made me wanna upvote more.

Good thing I read the whole discussion before commenting because of the strongly worded mod post which I would have violated…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

This one didn't really do much for me - the core concept was pretty bland and lacked depth, I don't see any reason for me to continue thinking about it once I've closed the page. The dialogue was also pretty painful, with the POI having a Bugs Bunny-esque level of vocal characterisation and the interviewer responding obediently to the interviewee's prompts. "I remember because it happened" "What happened?" is not a correspondence that occurs in any realistic conversation.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Geograph
License: CC BY-SA 2.0
Title: Gap between houses, Glenyards Road
Author: Richard Webb
Release year: 2009

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Looks good to me!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Hey, that's a neat skip. Now that Roget has created a hub for all things Psi-7, a link can be made in the containment procedures there. Additionally, the spatial anomaly here reminds me of that which is seen in SCP-2147, which is also coincidentally located in Florida. Its not unreasonable for a Researcher for the SCPF to make this observation and look into whether the two are related, and if more of these kinds of things are going to pop up and turn Floridaland into a space time Swiss cheese. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Any entrance to SCP-1967 is to be constructed over, and security personnel posing as homeowners are to be assigned to guard it. Residential homes connected to SCP-1967 are to be purchased through the Foundation realty front "Suburban Community Purchases" and be guarded in the same manner. Only personnel from MTF-ψ-7 "Home Improvement" are permitted to enter SCP-1967.

To this:

Any entrance to SCP-1967 is to be constructed over, and security personnel posing as homeowners are to be assigned to guard it. Residential homes connected to SCP-1967 are to be purchased through the Foundation realty front "Suburban Community Purchases" and be guarded in the same manner. Only personnel from MTF-ψ-7 "Home Improvement" are permitted to enter SCP-1967.

Changing this:

SCP-1967 is a spatial anomaly, currently known to exist in 3 locations: A suburban neighborhood in Plantation, FL, an office building in Miami, FL, and an apartment building in Miami, FL. It can be entered via a number of different entrances, with current means including a swimming pool, a rabbit hole, jumping a certain distance from a swing, and through approximately 22 side yards. As of 11/16/1987, approximately ██ separate entrances and exits have been found.

To this:

SCP-1967 is a spatial anomaly, currently known to exist in 3 locations: A suburban neighborhood in Plantation, FL, an office building in Miami, FL, and an apartment building in Miami, FL. It can be entered via a number of different entrances, with current means including a swimming pool, a rabbit hole, jumping a certain distance from a swing, and through approximately 22 side yards. As of 11/16/1987, approximately ██ separate entrances and exits have been found.1

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Similarity between the effects and location of this anomaly and SCP-2147 has been noted. Research into a potential shared origin is currently ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels… Woefully incomplete. I generally dislike items where there's literally no way to comprehend the hook without reading the comments.
Missing:
- Experimentation before and after the interview. Did the 'new' access points appear only when the guy tried using them or were they always there? How is a new one established to begin with?
- A simple [sic] at the first occurrence of yes, it's still shattering SoD for non-native speakers.
- The eternal "can we use it for storing stuff" like was done with the teleporting pool, city-in-a-symphony or the bunker-in-the-nasal-cavity. Because while localized narrowly, it seems to be far more stable than many other such storage solutions already in place.

I know it's part of the hook, but one other thing confsed me between reading the article and reading the comments: were the "guards" REALLY only SCP agents? The way the interviewer acta does not make that sufficiently clear, made me think there was a "only children can perceive them" third party involved which the subject confused and conflated in his memory with SVP operatives.

item.title: Staff Post -- Closed
item.content:encoded:
You are responding to a year old post, which would have been fine if you were adding to discussion. But you're not, because you're responding with something that's already been said.

Not only are you necroposting, but there is a Mod Post right above us that's saying "Stop responding to this post."

Please avoid doing this in the future.

Do not respond to this post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's some pretty extreme nitpicking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd have liked this if I'd got the Yggdrasil feeling from the article. As it is, I just got a sense of 'It's a weird space that sometimes connects places and sometimes doesn't.' I didn't think there was quite enough exploration of the concept and too much focus on one guy (to the extent that I thought he was supposed to be the anomaly in some way).

A shame, because I normally like childhood-connected SCPs, bringing back the sense of wonder and ignorance about the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha you said butt.

item.title: Mod Post
item.content:encoded:
You say "po-TAY-toe", I say "SHUT UP ABOUT THE PRONUNCIATION ALREADY."

Every. Single. Aspect of this "debate" has been made, repeatedly. Roget has clearly said that spelling it "root" was deliberate rather than a typo. Arguing that OMG HE'S WRONG because you personally pronounce it differently is NOT HELPFUL. The argument has been going on literally from the moment the article was posted and it has had exactly zero effect.

I am putting a stop to discussing/arguing about the pronunciation, in my capacity as a Moderator. Continuing this particular topic is now grounds to have the thread locked. Repeatedly continuing to ignore this stop order, by ANYBODY, will be discussed with other Mods/Admins as possible grounds for banning. If you want to discuss other aspects of this article, continue to do so, but the pronunciation issue is hereby locked.

You have been warned.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded:
So I did a bit more research on this subject because we actually know a lot more about this than we were taking into account. The subject is from Plantation, Florida. Which is just outside of Fort Lauderdale in the very southern part of Florida.

Dialect surveys of the United States show a marked difference between the speech patterns of citizens of Northern Florida as opposed to Southern Florida.

Notably the Southern area of Florida seems to be exempt from the more traditional "Southern Dialect" that exists in the Northern "Panhandle" area.

According to dialect surveys and independent responses it appears that if our subject is from Plantation, Florida he would have likely pronounced Route as (Rowt). As opposed to the Panhandle area where they do indeed prounounce it "Rute".

Now I find it highly unlikely that given the current evidence that the foundation researcher in charge of submitting this report did so incorrectly.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded: As I said "DIALECT" has something to do with it. A regional accent may affect pronunciation. But an accent doesn't make two completely different words homophones. Just because someone says.. "Let me axe you a question" due to their inability to pronounce the work Ask correctly does not make the words Ask and Axe homophones.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded: Hey, at least you might get some upvotes from the attention.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded: It was the best part of writing the article, for me.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded:
I pronounce root and route the same, and many people I know do.

Edit: I also find it hilarious and sad that we're still arguing about this three months later.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded:
It's regional as far as I know - I myself have always pronounced route as rhyming with drought, bout, lout, etc.

In any event, there's absolutely no reason why the person copying down the interview text would transcribe the word as "roots" in this context - "back routes" is an established turn of phrase, so even if the child pronounces "route" as if it rhymes with "boot" or "moot", he wouldn't have any reason to conclude that the child meant to say "root".

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded:
They're homophones where I live. Usually. It's kind of curious, because I drive on 'root' 9, but if there's construction, traffic may be 're-rowted'. I also 'rowt' my cable through the living room wall.

In the sense the word is used here, though, it's 'root'.

item.title: Re: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded: I'm a foreigner, so take it with a grain of salt, but just about everytime I heard routers mentioned it was pronounced as rooter would be. (Asides from people who read it as written)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Asides from the roots/routes discussion (which at least to my ear are homophones, hence concurring with Voct et al.) , this article isn't particularly intriguing - we have oodles and oodles of anomalous spaces already, including that SCP which lets you access what the author described as the -1 level of the city it's located in.

Basically, these kiddos found Yggdrasil.

I'd like this interpretation, now if it was actually somehow coming from the article.

item.title: Not a homophone
item.content:encoded:
Root and Route are not homophones.. Period. End of discussion.

I buy a ROUTER (rowter) not a ROOTER (rooter)

Trees have roots (roots) not routes (rowts)

You call Roto-Rooter not Roto-Router

OU makes an OW, or an AW sound depending on what letter follows the OU sound.

(OW sounds) Out, Ouch, About, Trout, Doubt, Lout, Account, Flounder, Founder, Mound, Pound

(AW sounds) Ought, Sought, Bought, Fought

Dialect is the only reason why someone would ever CONFUSE Route with Root. And that's only if the person doing the recording treats them as homophones, not because they actually are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …How does capitalization solve the problem of "roots" not being the spelling that someone transcribing the interview would use?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'back roots' ==> 'Back Roots'

Problem solved.

…Nah, that looks dumb.
I like the little nickname that the place had, where it came from, and the implication that the masked kid thought that the Foundation was part of the SCP. The article is quaint and somewhat nostalgic. I like it. I just hate the roots/routes discussion here. >___>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two(ish) big problems, for me:

1) Things can be placed (I assume through open windows/doors) in the buildings that appear to be along the edge, but you can't enter one with your whole body because "You just can't". Plus, nobody has done, or apparently even expressed any interest in, experimentation along those lines.

This is not believable. It's certainly possible that nobodys gotten around to testing it yet (perhaps it's a busy time at the old lab), but not that nobodys even thought of it, mention of which would appear in this document.

2) The "Root/Route" thing makes suspension of disbelief very hard, with no apparent benefit, and the author seems to be aware of this but unconcerned about it.

The spelling issue would not be difficult to address, and for this to be plausibly an SCP document, it would have to be, because, as has been mentioned, there's no reason at all for a transcript of a verbal conversation to use the "Root" spelling. It's not that it strains plausibility, it's that it flatly defies it.

The first thing a submission must do to warrant an upvote is to be credible as an SCP document. This isn't, as interesting an idea as it may be. What puzzles me most is that it would be trivial to change that.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm theorizing that in any attempt to enter a building through either window or door, they will be shut and locked with no way to open them and impervious to any forces strong enough to normally open them. This place within the 'roots' is a convergence in space of some kind and perhaps recognizes living things and knows to prevent access from anything other than its intended exit points.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this an implied accent? I've heard people speak this way before is why I ask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like all this could be cleared up with just a footnote on the first instance of "roots" that says nothing but [DATA EXPUNGED].

It confused me a bit, myself, but what really bothered me was the part where "Subjects … will not be able to enter them." It comes across to me as very you-can't-get-ye-flask. Why can't people enter buildings from the Roots? What happens when they try?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it is permitted; but do I really want to be that guy?

I appreciate the official rulings. I'll sleep on it and see if I'm still irked in the morning.
Too bad this is otherwise quite good, it would be simpler if I wasn't conflicted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You forgot the [/adminhat], I'll leave that here for ya.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can downvote an article for any reason pertaining to its contents, including "its SCP number is a multiple of 17" or "there are too many 'p's and not enough 'f's".

[adminhat] If anyone actually ever downvotes an article for those reasons I am going to come down on them like a hobnail boot on a cockroach for it.

The Root/Route thing is a different matter because that actually does pertain to the contents and context of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It'd only be malicious if you downvoted because you don't like the author.

You can downvote an article for any reason pertaining to its contents, including "its SCP number is a multiple of 17" or "there are too many 'p's and not enough 'f's".

If you read a comment from the author that says "this SCP is based on my murder fantasies of killing everyone at my job" and you can't bring yourself to like that particular SCP any more, that wouldn't be a malicious downvote. A malicious downvote would be if you then went around to all the other SCPs written by that author and downvoted them without even rereading them to see if you like or dislike them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, and up-voted, but had the same word confusion as everybody else. The discussion has spoiled this article for me and I am debating a strong urge to go back and down-vote.

This is a common problem with noob D&D players, and a personal pet peeve. It is essential to separate player (author) knowledge from character (Foundation, in-universe) knowledge. Mixing this up ruins the game for everyone else. Refusing to make the minor tweak that would patch this hole in the plot, or even acknowledge that there is a valid issue, is irritating to me on a personal, gut-reaction level.

I don't know if that would count as a malicious down-vote, or is it justified because I legitimately don't like the piece anymore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like much SCPs that you have to read the comments to get them. They should act pretty much standalone. And taken from that point of view, this is pretty meh. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't understand the "roots" thing, but Djoric's explanation is now my headcanon of this article.
But yeah, it's going to be a sticking point for a lot of people unless you imply some deeper meaning or shallow reasoning for the spelling.
+1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's supposed to be that way, seen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A concept for you, it's called SLANG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm just stupid and not seeing the real reason for this, but this has been bugging me…
From the interview…

So the guards couldn't get me, seen?

Why is "Seen" after the comma, and ending with a question mark? Is it supposed to be ", see?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really lovely article, I enjoyed it a lot. It's left me feeling incredibly nostalgic - thinking back on all the special little paths we used to take that would take you to another world, the old waste ground we'd play on. I like that "roots" is a play on "routes" - it's exactly the kind of phrase children end up using (for example the "skape" tunnel myself and a portion of my friends were adamant to dig under the school gates) and I feel it adds more than it detracts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why don't someone fix it as 'branches' and imply the article's words are changed to tree-stuff?
/sarcasm

item.title: Re: The really real reason why it's "roots" and not "routes"
item.content:encoded: And that's fine, but unless something is added to the article making that clear readers with otherwise good intentions will likely continue to miss your intent and "correct" it. Without providing some context it's really quite confusing, both from an in-universe and out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I think we've gotten to the root of the problem here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was pretty obvious when every single instance was spelled "Roots"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article should make it clear that that's what the author knows, then. As is, there's no reason for the reader to see that and assume "Oh, so it's actually a metaphysical tree that binds the multiverse together" rather than "Oh, so the Foundation hires researchers who are bad at words."

item.title: The really real reason why it's "roots" and not "routes"
item.content:encoded: It's because when I was a kid, we had an area that a bunch of houses on the block backed up to, and we used it to play imagination games. We called the area "the back roots." Since this article is based on that game, I kept the name. That's all there is to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, but I agree with Voct on the "routes" vs. "roots" thing. (Maybe it's just because I pronounce "route" to rhyme with "out"?)

EDIT: also, this article didn't really grab me until the end of the transcript… maybe I'm in Critic Mode right now or something, but it was just kinda "meh" until the tail-end. Make of that what you will, I'm still upvoting :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But there is nothing else that says that, anywhere. It doesn't fit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All dat nitpicking up in here…

Weird enough for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm gonna take a stab at this and say that it really has nothing to do with the mask. I see the mask as just a random object he wore so that no one saw his face. It became inactive when no one was using it, because he went off to college and everyone else had been given amnesiacs.

Edit: After reading over the log again I realized I had misread. It might actually be the mask.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Come on down to Containment Beach! You'll never want to leave!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviewer wrote roots instead of routes. This means that they know they're talking about roots, not routes.

item.title: Anomalies
item.content:encoded:
Anyway, leaving aside the "roots" / "routes" mess, the anomalous factors include:

this field is accessible from multiple cities

the field borders on several buildings, but if you're in the field, you can't actually go into the buildings. You can observe them, and put things in them, but not go in.

if you're in the field, people in the buildings can't see you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No additional containment beaches

Fail correction is fail. I think I'll just fix it myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see that implication ANYWHERE in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct. The in-universe author of this article has no reason to be sly and drop hints to his bosses instead of saying "it's a metaphysical cosmic tree".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, well, carry on then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For further reading, the FAQ has a blurb about this. Kind of. In a nutshell it's author preference as to whether to use amnesiac or amnestic. (Though the latter is the medical term for a drug that induces amnesia. )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amnestics cause amnesia, amnesiacs have amnesia.

Right on with the "brach" thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No additional containment braches have been reported as of 12/12/1960, and all subjects involved have been administered Class-E amnestics.

Not sure if typo or intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Smapti was referring to the in-character writer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gonna disagree with you there, author knowledge is not the same as Foundation researcher knowledge.
